notes.anchor: driving off the spleen and regulating the circulation
notes.note: In older medicine the spleen was held to be the seat of melancholy and ill temper, so to drive it off meant to dispel gloom. Ishmael's half-joke is that a sea voyage is his cure for depression, setting his blood and his low spirits moving again.

Older British schools called a low-ranking assistant teacher an usher — not someone who seats people. The sense suits this worn, threadbare figure forever among old grammars and lexicons: a junior schoolmaster of languages, not a doorkeeper.

Once a common adverb meaning "immediately, at once" (from the older sense of being unable to hold back), unrelated to the modern bodily meaning. It marks how anything entering the whale's mouth goes straight down without the least delay.

In older slang a "hunks" was a crabbed, stingy, bad-tempered old fellow — a miser. Pinning the label on the sea-captain casts him as a gruff, mean-spirited skinflint who snaps out orders, not just a man advanced in years.

Once a standard collective noun for the ordinary people of a society, taken together and set against its rulers or nobility. Here it names the common mass that, unawares to those in charge, quietly steers the very leaders who imagine they command it.

In older usage a toper was a habitual hard drinker, a confirmed drunkard. Here the gin overpowers even the most seasoned of them, fresh off a long voyage, so they start leaping and carrying on wildly.

Originally a farrago was a mash of mixed grain fed to cattle, and the word came to mean any confused jumble or hodgepodge. Here it names the landlord's muddled, baffling run of talk, which leaves the narrator enraged at a string he cannot make sense of.

As a noun, this names a long wooden bench, typically with a high back and arms and often set near a hearth, seating several people. Here it is the carved seat Ishmael takes in the inn, not the modern verb meaning to sit or resolve.

Originally describing land covered in bushes, woods, or thickets, the word transfers that wild, overgrown quality to the men's beards, marking them as thick, untrimmed, and matching the unshorn, shaggy crew being described.

In older usage, the dregs or muddy sediment that settles to the bottom of wine and other liquors. Here the speaker casts the physical body as that leftover residue, the coarse remnant beneath a finer, more spiritual self.

To essay something, as a verb, is to attempt or try it — unrelated to the noun for a school paper. Paired here with the word “tries,” the older sense shows the speaker straining without success to force a smile he cannot make convincing.

An older word for greed, especially a craving for money — unrelated to Cupid or love despite the look of it. Here the captain's avarice is what makes him chase wrongdoing only in the poor, who lack the means to buy their way out.

An archaic verb meaning to hurry or hasten toward a place. Here the drunken reveler hastens off to bed. Outside the surviving phrase "hie thee," the word is now unfamiliar, so a modern reader can miss the sense of urgent motion it carries.

An older verb meaning to spoil, corrupt, or render impure or defective. Here Ishmael casts Queequeg's royal blood as fine but degraded by the habits of his early life. Formal and now rare, with little hint of its meaning in its roots.

An old idiom meaning "for the present, for the time being." The word "nonce" survives almost nowhere else and carries no separate sense a student would know. Here it frames Queequeg's plan to keep sailing as a temporary choice, weighed against his eventual return home.

An old past tense of "cleave" in its sense of to cling or stick fast, not the unrelated "cleave" meaning to split — and not the spice. The verb marks how closely Ishmael held to Queequeg, an attachment reinforced by the barnacle comparison that follows.

Old colloquial slang for a thoroughly good, dependable, first-rate fellow, related to the winning "trump card." In this line the whole crew rate Queequeg an admirable, trustworthy man. A modern reader hears only the surname or a card-game term and misses the high praise.

A stiver was a small Dutch coin of trifling worth — pocket-change of the old maritime world. Saying he would not have to pay even this much is a way of insisting his board cost him nothing at all, the same as today's "not one cent."

In older usage, to "cant" is to mouth pious or moralizing talk insincerely, parading religious phrases without real feeling. Here Peleg hurls the word at scripture-quoting Bildad to ridicule him as a sanctimonious hypocrite. The modern senses ("can't," or a tilt) miss this preacherly meaning entirely.

In older usage a castor was not a furniture wheel but a tabletop frame holding cruets of condiments — oil, vinegar, mustard, and the like. Tending these is what occupies Mrs. Hussey, and the mustard-pot and vinegar-cruet in her hands are the very bottles such a stand carries.

Originally sailors' slang for frolicking or horseplay in the rigging, the word came to mean fooling about or playing tricks rather than speaking in earnest. Bildad charges that Ishmael is toying with him instead of giving a serious answer.

Originally a sudden, unexpected stroke arriving after the trouble seemed over — like a last thunderclap once the storm has passed. Here it names the delayed reckoning a newly pious sailor fears: a punishment that might fall after death if a whale should kill him.

An old verb meaning to talk idly and at length, babbling without weight or sense. Bildad rebukes Peleg for running on so carelessly and irreverently about death, when Peleg of all people knows the true fear of it.

Originally an apple-seller (from "costard," a kind of apple), this is a street peddler hawking fruit and vegetables from a cart or stall. Grouped here with grocers and bakers, it names one of the everyday provision trades a whaler is cut off from for years at sea.

An old adjective meaning "various, several, sundry" — distinct from and unrelated to the noun "diver." Modifies "odds and ends" to mean miscellaneous things of many different kinds being carried aboard, not anything to do with diving.

An old adverb meaning "moreover" or "for all that," used to add a final point or concede one. Here it pivots from her determined, tireless nature to a contrasting trait: stern and unflagging, yet for all that very kindhearted. Reads like "nevertheless."

In old slang a person's "traps" are portable belongings — luggage, kit, gear. Here the two men haul their packed things down to be sent aboard the ship, even while planning to keep sleeping ashore until the last possible night before sailing.

A "stave" is a single verse or stanza of a song, and "psalmody" is the singing of psalms or sacred hymns. So a gloomy hymn-verse is meant: pious Bildad chants one to set the work pace while the crew answer with a bawdy tune instead.

In old sea usage, the offing is the deep open water well off the coast, the distant sea where a vessel rides clear of land. Reaching it meant the ship had worked far enough out that the harbor pilots, who guide vessels near shore, were no longer needed.

Here "scout" is an old verb meaning to mock or dismiss with scorn — to scoff at — unrelated to scouting or reconnaissance. The world derides whale hunters, yet (Ishmael claims) honors them all the same.

In older usage this means a conception, notion, or commonly held opinion — not vanity or an elaborate metaphor. Danger swells the public's idea of how grand soldiering is, the point being that a whale's tail unnerves veterans more than cannon fire.

An archaic and literary term meaning powerful or mighty, once common in formal English. Having listed whaling's wealth and far-flung fleets, Ishmael argues that no trade could amass so much unless a real, forceful power were at work within it.

In older usage "mean" means low, base, or of little worth, so to esteem someone "meanly" is to regard him with low esteem rather than to treat him cruelly. Here it marks the poor opinion ordinary people hold of a man who oils his hair.

An old adjective meaning fit, suitable, or proper — not the everyday verb a reader expects. "It is therefore but meet" thus says it is only right and fitting to record here who the harpooneers were and which headsman each served.

An old colloquial intensifier, formed from "plague," meaning confoundedly annoying, vexatious, or troublesome. Here it marks the night's puzzling business as a bothersome, hard-to-untangle matter to be reconsidered in daylight.

Once colloquial slang clipped from "physiognomy," a phiz meant a person's face or facial expression, often a peculiar or comical one. Here the word marks Stubb's alarm at the strange-looking face he encounters, a look odd enough to make him recoil.

A ferule was a flat rod or ruler that old-time schoolmasters used to strike pupils' hands as punishment. The Thrasher whale wields its tail the same way, beating its foes — a comparison the surrounding lines carry on into talk of schoolmasters.

Hustings were the raised platform where election candidates were nominated and harangued the crowd, so the word came to mean the whole noisy business of campaigning for office. Melville says the truly great are kept clear of this public scramble for fame and acclaim.

A small round shield gripped in the fist to ward off blows, distinct from a larger arm-strapped shield. Here it caps a grim joke: a waiter serving these ravenous harpooneers needs no napkin draped on his arm but armor to fend off the diners.

In older English "carking" meant gnawing or distressing, from "cark," to fret or be anxious. So these are the wearing, burdensome worries of ordinary land life — the everyday troubles that the dreamy young men hope to leave behind by going to sea.

An older word for a fever marked by violent fits of shivering and shaking. Ahab flings it as an insult at the boy whose trembling, unsteady hands keep spilling the drink, naming him a shivering-fit made flesh.

To "twig" something was old colloquial slang meaning to notice it sharply and catch on to its meaning all at once. Stubb uses it to say he instantly perceived that Ahab had likewise taken hold of Starbuck, reading it the moment he looked at the man.

Originally a fillip was a sharp flick of a fingertip snapped from under the thumb; the verb came to mean to stimulate or rouse with a quick jolt. Set against "deadening," it marks how the same wine stupefies some drinkers but spurs others to life.

In old British slang (sailors' and thieves' cant), a fellow or chap — a man of any rank. Here the English seaman uses it admiringly, ranking Ahab a grand old man worth following; the word carries no connection to a coastal inlet.

To "bruit" once meant to report or rumor something, to spread it as news by word of mouth. Here it marks how these disasters, however little talked about back on land, were nonetheless common occurrences out in the fishery.

Once meant a magician's sleight of hand, from French for "light of hand" — any quick, deceptive conjuring. Here the frost and air work like a cruel illusionist, transforming polar ice into a grinning graveyard of monuments and crosses before the sailor's eyes.

Originally a tenth part of one's produce owed to the church, "tithe" came to mean a tenth and then loosely any small share. To lack one tithe of something is to fall short of even a tiny fraction of it.

An archaic verb meaning to scoff at or reject with scorn, unrelated to the modern "scout" who reconnoiters. Here it names the reaction Melville expects from landsmen: they would jeer at the whale's story, dismissing it as a far-fetched fable rather than fact.

An old verb, to gainsay, meant to speak against, deny, or contradict — built on the prefix "gain-," meaning "against." Here it marks the claim that something recorded in solid, established history cannot easily be denied or argued away.

As a verb this once meant to dismiss someone from office or command, often in disgrace—a military term for breaking an officer. Punning on "cash," it imagines the crew's greed itself stripping Ahab of his command.

In formal rhetoric, this is the opening section of a speech or oration, meant to introduce the topic and win over the listeners. Applied to Stubb's rough pep talk to his rowers, the grand term mock-inflates a crude bit of shouted encouragement into a dignified address.

"Wont" once meant one's habit or custom, so something "unwonted" is unaccustomed, outside ordinary practice. Marking the hour this way signals that a whale-cry at this time of night breaks the usual routine, since whales were rarely chased after dark.

An old adverb meaning with full force or at full strength — violently, hard. Here the wind climbs to that pitch just as the captain loses his trumpet, so his shouted words are blown away and cannot reach the other ship.

An archaic term for clothing or dress, common in older devotional and scriptural English. Here it names the sailors' garments, so torn and patched after nearly four years at sea that they read as the rough hides of beasts.

A now-rare word for fussy, overly precise, and fastidious about trivial details — a cousin of "finicky." It casts the two passing ships as snobbish dandies who, instead of a greeting, silently nitpick each other's rig with prissy, hairsplitting criticism.

An archaic verb, to cozen meant to cheat or deceive by trickery. Calling the prompting spirit a cozening fiend casts it as a deceiving devil that lured Radney into provoking a dangerous, exhausted man against his own interest.

Historically a small, slender dagger or stiletto meant for stabbing. Used here for Steelkilt's "unflinching" stare, it recasts his eyes as a blade driven steadily into the mate's eye, all menace and no movement.

A now-archaic adjective meaning Roman Catholic, used by Protestants with a dismissive or hostile edge. The "injunctions of secrecy" here are imagined as binding in the solemn, ritual way associated with the Catholic confessional, sworn so seriously they could only be broken in sleep.

In old grain-harvesting, the top sheaf set to crown and finish a stack; by extension, the crowning or final addition. Here it casts Cuvier's drawing as the topping-off mistake that completes a whole run of bungled whale pictures.

Originally a long, flat stiffening strip — of bone, wood, or whalebone — slid down the front of a woman's corset to hold it straight. Here sailors shaped such stays from Right Whalebone, then carved and engraved them as keepsakes during idle hours at sea.

Once an adjective (not only the familiar noun) meaning imbued or charged with something, always paired with "with." So "instinct... with the same sort of life" means animated through and through by life — Melville doubts these rock-like whales are as fully alive as a dog or horse.

A burgher was a citizen or freeman of a town, usually a solid, well-off member of the merchant middle class; "portly" adds the older sense of stout and dignified. Together they paint a plump, contented townsman, the image to which the fat, slow-spouting whale is likened as it leisurely smokes its pipe.

An old naval and merchant-marine word for being knocked down in rank or rating as a punishment, with the loss of standing and pay that followed. Here the harpooneers who miss their throw are not merely cursed but officially demoted aboard ship.

In older English this names a short, abrupt thing said aloud — an exclamation or sudden utterance, with no bodily sense. Here it labels Fleece's muttered parting complaint as he limps off, casting his grumble as a brief burst of speech.

An archaic verb meaning to compose and put into written words, often something formal. Here the Secretary indites his circulars, that is, drafts and writes them out. Distinct from the look-alike "indict," to charge with a crime.

A "shamble" was a butcher's table or stall, and by extension a slaughterhouse or place of carnage. Here the bloody work of cutting up the whale makes the ship one. Unrelated to the modern verb meaning to walk awkwardly.

"Ween" is an archaic verb meaning to think, suppose, or imagine — unrelated to "win" or "wean." Dropped in as an aside, it works like "I'd guess" or "I imagine," lending the remark an old-fashioned, mock-solemn air.

An old measure: as land area, a quarter-acre; as length, a rod of about five and a half yards. Coined into "square" and then "cubic" forms, it stretches the unit into vast volumes, sizing the swarms of sharks and birds as immense teeming masses.

Originally means white or gray, especially the grayness of old age or frost; by extension, ancient and venerable. Here it describes the whale's head where pale mosses have whitened it, lending the creature an aged, time-worn aspect that suits Ahab's address to it as wise and venerable.

Two stock purgatives of the era: calomel, a mercury-chloride powder, and jalap, a dried Mexican root, both given to violently empty the bowels. Naming them turns the cup of mild ginger-water into a dose of nasty druggist's physic, scorned as no fit drink for a chilled man.

"Darbies" was old slang for handcuffs or fetters, so to be in double-darbies is to have both wrists ironed. Stubb says the governor ought to clap the devil in shackles like any criminal, yet instead lets him roam loose.

In earlier centuries, this was a frame of hoops worn under a woman's skirt to spread it into a broad bell or dome shape. Whalebone supplied the springy support such frames needed, so when this fashion ruled, demand for the bone surged.

Once a long, costly outer cloak or coat lined or trimmed with fur or silk. Here it supplies the image of a smooth, rich silken lining, matched to the pearl-coloured membrane covering the inner surface of the Sperm Whale's case.

A camphorated tincture of opium, this was a 19th-century medicine-chest staple dosed for pain and especially bellyache or diarrhea. Stubb calls for it on seeing the ailing old whale's gurgling distress, treating the beast as if it had a human stomachache.

A light, open two-wheeled gig drawn by a single horse, fashionable in the early 1800s for its speed and flimsy build. Imagining one hitched to a bolting elephant gives the image of a fragile carriage dragged at wild speed, wheel-spokes blurring and the rider nearly flung out — Stubb's picture of a boat fast to a fleeing whale.

A broad, slow two-masted Dutch boat once worked for cod-fishing on the North Sea. Stubb flings the term at the German skipper's vessel as contempt, equating the rival ship with a plodding, ill-mannered fishing tub rather than a proper whaler.
Once a common word for a small metal drinking can or cup, often tin. Stubb proposes holding one up to the spouting whale to catch the jet and pass the cup around in a mock toast.

Once a "zone" was a belt or girdle worn around the waist (the original Greek sense), and by extension the waist itself. The elephant, having handed flowers to the young women, touches them at the waist where such a sash would sit.

Old shipbuilding term for pig-iron or scrap metal stowed permanently in a hull's bottom solely to weigh it down and steady the vessel. Here it marks the dead-weight ballast an ordinary ship carries, against which the whaler's hold of useful stores is set.

Once a small drawstring purse or pouch that women carried, with contents cinched tightly inside. Naming the womb a "maternal reticule" casts it as that snug little bag, where the unborn whale lay folded and cramped before birth.

A genteel old term (from French embonpoint) for a well-fed plumpness or fullness of figure. Here it grants the female whales a natural, hereditary stoutness, framing their bulk in polite, flattering terms rather than as mere size.

An old noun for spells of gloom or low spirits, now surviving only in "down in the dumps." Pairing fits of dejection with mounting age, it marks how the aging bull whale turns heavy-hearted and weary, his appetite for the harem fading.

Short for "criminal conversation," an old legal term for adultery and the civil suit a husband could bring against his wife's lover, treating her almost as property. Erskine's example likens a runaway wife to a whale a man can later sue to reclaim.
Trover, in old common law, was a suit to recover the value of personal property wrongfully taken or kept. Melville welds it to whale to name a mock-grand courtroom fight over a disputed catch.

Old slang for cheating or swindling someone, especially out of something valuable. Stubb uses it to admit, in a word the French captain cannot follow, that he has tricked the man into casting off the prized whale so Stubb can claim its ambergris.

Once meant a small bunch of fragrant flowers held to the nose for its sweet scent. Applying it to a rotting, foul-smelling dead whale is heavy sarcasm, naming the stinking carcass as if it were a sweet posy.

A scented, waxy ointment once rubbed into the hair to make it shine and smell sweet — the forerunner of modern hair pomade. Here it appears among the perfumed luxury goods to which ambergris lent its fragrance.

Once meant dirty and slovenly, the habits of a "slattern" (an untidy woman). It names the slander against whaling here: that the trade is a filthy, careless business, a charge the narrator means to refute.

A "tyro," from the Latin for a raw military recruit, meant any beginner or untrained newcomer in a craft. Here it marks the inexperienced hands who find the dark, slippery blubber-room terrifying because they have not yet learned to work it safely.

In older usage, the prescribed robes a clergyman wears while officiating church rites. Melville drapes the lowly mincer in his whale-skin garb as though it were sacred dress, casting this greasy laborer as a comic priest of blubber.

Once meant a small, poor bed or a thin straw mattress laid on the floor, not a wooden shipping platform. Here it marks the merchant sailor's meanness of life: he gropes in the dark to a wretched cot for want of light.

Once a name for fine smooth linen, woven first in the Netherlands and prized for bedsheets and shirts. The freshly scrubbed sailors come up like grooms rising from the costliest linen sheets, not from the country itself.

An old sailors' expression for being far gone in drink, well on the way to drunkenness. The wounded captain uses it to mock the supposedly teetotal surgeon, who plied him with hot rum toddies and packed him off to bed thoroughly intoxicated at three in the morning.

Flip was a tavern and shipboard drink of ale or rum with sugar (sometimes egg), heated and made to foam by plunging in a red-hot iron. Here it marks the rough hospitality of a midnight visit aboard another whaler off Patagonia.

Old colloquial term for the head or skull (unrelated to the modern wall light). The squabbling priests are rapping each other on the head with their measuring sticks, an image the echoing skull nearby plays off of.

notes.anchor: untagging the points of his hose
notes.note: "Points" were short laces tipped with metal tags that tied breeches, hose, and doublets together in old dress; to untag them was to loosen these fastenings. Here it extends the figure of stripping the whale down to his bare skeleton.

An archaic verb meaning to noise abroad, report, or spread by talk. Here it marks the reason Peleg put about for the captain's seclusion as merely the widely circulated account, one the narrator judges inadequate rather than the true cause.

A spavin is a disease of a horse's hock joint that leaves the animal permanently lame; to be spavined is thus to be crippled in the leg. Here the carpenter speaks of Ahab as a hard driver who has worn out his own legs as such a man wears out his horses.

In older usage a "poser" is a question hard enough to "pose," or perplex, anyone who hears it, leaving them stumped for an answer. Replying to Ahab's riddle about feeling a leg that is gone, the carpenter confesses the puzzle utterly baffles him.
To prate is to talk on and on idly, to chatter tediously about trifles. Ahab casts Starbuck's persistent talk of the ship's owners as empty babble, mere noise rather than a claim on his conscience.

An old sense of "wax" meaning to grow or become, as the moon waxes larger before it wanes. Here it marks Starbuck swelling into boldness enough to talk back, even as he still bends to the order he was given.

Originally from Latin "without care," a sinecure is a post that pays a salary or confers honor while demanding little or no real work. Whaling has none of them: here every gain in rank only brings harder, more dangerous labor.

Crape (crepe) was a dull, crinkled black silk worn or displayed as a sign of mourning for the dead; to go "in crape" is to go clothed in grief. Here it marks the broken blacksmith as a wanderer steeped in sorrow over his lost wife and children.

In older usage a "competency" was a modest but sufficient income, enough to cover life's needs without want; "care" here means worry, so "care-killing" means worry-ending. Together the words name an inheritance large enough to leave the survivors free of money anxiety.

In the 19th century a "lucifer" was a friction match that flared into flame the instant it was struck. Calling the flitting, fire-tending figure a bunch of them likens his quick, darting movements to so many matches catching light at once.

A fusee was a match with an oversized head built to catch and keep its flame even in wind, used to light pipes or fuses. The comparison casts the Parsee as something that draws and holds fire as readily as such a match, linking him to flame and gunpowder.

An older word for prayers, especially ones spoken or chanted aloud, from Latin oratio. Here it casts the sounds rising into the sunset air as devotions twining upward as the whale dies, readying the line's turn to the evening hymns of vespers.

From the old verb "to wont," to be in the habit of: accustomed, customary, usual. Here it marks Ahab's noon sun-sighting as his regular daily routine, the everyday measurement by which he fixes the ship's latitude.

A flambeau is a flaming torch, often carried to light a person's way at night; the -eaux ending marks the French plural. Here the lightning is imagined hurling down such torches of fire to illuminate Ahab's way to his post on deck.

A dialect term (kin to "hurrah") for a loud, confused uproar or commotion. Here it names the howling racket of the storm in the rigging overhead, which Ahab then sardonically likens to the noise of a bellyache.

A smack was a small single-masted sailing vessel used for inshore fishing or short-haul coastal trade. Being likened to the skipper of one casts Ahab as master of a lowly little craft, not the commander of a great whaler — an insult to his stature.

Old slang "togs" meant clothes, so long-togs named a landsman's fine, long-tailed dress coat as against a sailor's short working jacket. Here it marks the genteel shore garment that seamen mock — though Stubb argues its long tail would shed rain better in a storm.

A beaver was a tall dress hat of beaver felt, the fashionable headgear of a gentleman ashore; to "drive down" one meant to jam or press it firmly on. Paired with a swallowtail coat, it marks the shift from rough sailor's clothes to formal landsman's attire.

"Sooth" was an old noun meaning truth, surviving now only inside "soothsayer," a truth-teller; so this idiom means "in truth" or "truly." It concedes that the sinking cask offers the drowned sailor a pillow, but truly a hard one.

A crupper is the leather strap that loops under a horse's tail to keep its harness from sliding; to be "cruppered" with something is to be strapped or saddled to it. The carpenter casts himself as a beast harnessed to the grim job of the coffin.

A bandbox was a thin, lightweight box for storing hats or detachable collars (the "bands" of a shirt). Here the carpenter belittles the coffin he has built by comparing it to such flimsy, throwaway packaging, too slight to be taken seriously as a resting place.

An old intensifier meaning "thorough," "downright," or "out-and-out," almost always pinned to something disreputable (an arrant knave, an arrant fool). Here it brands the carpenter a complete, unmitigated scamp who shapes legs, coffins, and life-buoys alike.

"Hap" is an old noun meaning chance, luck, or accident — the root that survives in "happen," "perhaps," and "mishap." So "by a mere hap" means by sheer accident, marking that only blind chance has turned a symbol of death into one of rescue.

In older usage "suit" names a petition or earnest plea — the act of asking or entreating (kin to "sue" and "pursue"), not garments or litigation. So an "earnest suit" is a heartfelt request pressed on someone, here the appeal the strange captain makes to Ahab.

An old past-tense form of "dare," meaning "dared." Here Ahab says his own natural heart "durst not"—dared not—even attempt the thing some hidden force now drives him to do, the doubled "durst not so much as dare" sharpening the sense of what he once would never have ventured.
An archaic, poetic contraction of "margin," meaning an edge, border, or rim. Here it names the outer rim of the bald, crater-like patch on Ahab's head, where a ring of scorched hair still grows.

Once meant a large merchant ship, often richly laden (from Ragusa, a busy trading port). Here it supplies the image of a grand painted hull, against which the broken lance standing up from the whale's back looks like a flagstaff on such a vessel.

Originally a sailor's word for low cloud, mist, or spray driven fast before a gale. Here the recoiling Channel waves clear the Eddystone's summit not as solid water but as this flying sheet of wind-blown spray.

Older usage: a fagot is a bundle of sticks or iron and steel rods bound together for carrying or burning. Here it names the snarled cluster of harpoon and lance irons cut free and dropped overboard as a single tangled mass of metal.
A 19th-century name for yellow fever, after the dark, blood-tinged vomiting of its fatal stage. Ahab flings the disease as a curse on whoever may have let the Parsee slip away.

"Raiment" is an archaic word for clothing or dress, and "sable" is an old poetic and heraldic term for black. Combined, they name the dead Parsee's black garments, here torn to shreds as his corpse is bound against the whale.

An old verb sense of "fag" meant to wear out or exhaust through hard effort. Here it raises the possibility that the whale is simply tired from the three-day chase, offered as one reason it begins to slow down.

As a noun, "the main" is an old word for the open ocean, the high seas (surviving in "the Spanish Main"). Here it names the wide stretch of sea Ishmael drifts across, called soft and dirgelike for its quiet, funeral-song calm.
